questionText: Compare these two toy-shelf orders from left to right for Robot, Teddy, Ball, and Kite. The rules are: Teddy is left of Robot, Ball is right of Robot, Robot is not on an end, and Kite is not on the far right. Order A is Teddy, Robot, Ball, Kite. Order B is Kite, Teddy, Robot, Ball. Choose the best judgment about the two orders.
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can solve logical puzzles involving ordering, grouping, and elimination
issues: [difficulty_mismatch] | The item is labeled hard, but it only requires checking two given orders against four stated rules; this is closer to a medium task than a hard one. | The task has no non-trivial real-world context or reverse/error-analysis demand expected for a hard item. | Fix: Relabel the item as medium, or increase cognitive demand by asking students to determine all possible valid orders, identify an error in a student's reasoning, or justify why one order must work and another cannot. | Fix: If keeping hard difficulty, use distractors tied to specific rule-checking mistakes (for example, overlooking the 'not on an end' rule).

questionText: A snack basket holds 3 treats: crackers, cheese, apple slices, and juice. The rules are: crackers must go with cheese, apple slices cannot go with cheese, and juice can go with anything. One correct basket already has juice and apple slices inside. Choose the treat that must be added to make the basket follow every rule.
subject: Maths
learningObjective: Students can solve logical puzzles involving ordering, grouping, and elimination
issues: [difficulty_mismatch, clarity, concept_accuracy] | The item is labeled hard, but it can be solved in one or two direct elimination steps, so the cognitive demand is too low for hard. | The setup says the basket holds 3 treats from a listed set, but one option is 'another juice,' which conflicts with the listed unique treats and creates unnecessary ambiguity about whether duplicates are allowed. | Because the correct answer is 'none of the three treats can be added,' the item becomes more about parsing wording than doing substantial logical reasoning. | Fix: Relabel the item as medium or redesign it as a harder reverse-reasoning puzzle with multiple possible additions to evaluate or a claim to test. | Fix: Clarify whether each treat can be used only once. If duplicates are not allowed, remove 'another juice' and rewrite the stem/options so all answer choices are valid candidate additions. | Fix: Make the correct answer a specific treat or basket completion rather than a wording-based 'none' option, unless the puzzle explicitly teaches impossibility reasoning.
